scenario:
Readers Diary is a popular weekly magazine published for school children. Its

readership for the last 30 years has been school students, who benefited from

their well written articles on current affairs, general knowledge, stories

imparting moral values among many other age-appropriate articles for young

children. Schools facilitated the distribution of this magazine to interested

students, who had to pay an annual subscription fee to receive a copy each

week.

In the recent years, the access to similar information from the internet and social

media resulted in a drastic fall in readership for the magazine. Mr. Jay Pal, a

leading educationalist, is the founder and chief editor of this magazine. He has

observed that the readers are currently only passively assimilating information

from print and online sources. Due to the easy availability of information from

different sources, retention of interest to any one source in the long term has

become challenging. Therefore, in order to retain readership of Readers Diary,

he proposed that in addition to the regular content, the magazine would

contain contests and puzzles, for which cash prizes would be distributed to the

winner. These puzzles and contests would be based on the topics covered in

the magazine or even any of its previous issues. This makes reading the

magazine ‘Readers Diary’ an interactive pursuit rather than a passive exercise,

and can prevent the erosion of interest. Cash prizes would be substantial to

retain the interest of the student. Such prizes are not new in the industry, but

were until date not offered by school magazines like Readers Diary.

Moreover, instead of relying on schools alone to access the student subscribers,

he plans to make Readers Diary available online. Any interested reader, not just

students from selected schools can subscribe to the magazine by creating a

login account on its website. The content and features like prize winning

contests will be the same for both print and online versions.

Readers Diary plans to hire content creators as well as designers to develop

exceptionally well-designed content for its articles, blogs, and puzzles. The

content creators would be the judges who will declare the winner for puzzles

and contests. In addition, subscribers of both print and online version are

encouraged to submit articles, that if found interesting will appear in the

magazine. It plans to put in place the necessary IT infrastructure that can host

the magazine’s website that will connect it to its readers. Jay Pal opines that

with increased outreach, the readership base will increase, that leads to

economies of scale. To publicize the magazine, Readers Diary plans to run

advertisements on popular TV channels catering to children. Mr. Jay Pal and 2

of his other partners, who jointly own the magazine plan to infuse capital to

fund this expansion.

Book publishers of popular school textbooks and story books, classes that

conduct summer camps and other extra-curricular activities have evinced

interest in advertising in the magazine, both print and online versions. The cost

of subscription for the physical magazine that would be distributed through schools would increase from the current rate of `5,200 per year to `7,800 per

year. Online subscription would be free for the first 10 articles on its website

every year, not including contents and puzzles content, after which the reader has to pay ` 6,240 per year to access the rest of the content. Jay Pal wants to

build the business model such that subscription remains the primary source of

revenue for both print and online versions.

Before implementing the ideas, Jay Pal wants to understand the proposition

using models like Business Canvas model, Kano Model and other strategic tools

to ensure seamless execution of this critical project that could determine the

survival of the magazine.

scenario:
Nimbus Bike Limited (NBL) is a manufacturer of bikes in India. Demand for bikes

is high on account of a growing population primarily comprising of youth in the

age group of 18-30 years. NBL is the second largest bike manufacturer in India.

Competition is stiff in a market where the customers are highly price sensitive.

Until now the company has been following a standard costing methodology to

understand and manage costs. The management determines cost standards

that need to be met and reviews them annually. However, despite the MIS

reports indicating that the cost variances are under control and are not

unfavorable to the company, NBL’s bikes are not cost competitive in the market.

Therefore, Tarun Kumar the current CEO of the company wants to explore the

concept of using Kaizen costing understand and manage costs. Currently the cost of manufacturing is ` 60,000 per bike. On adopting the Kaizen costing

methodology, the management has set of goal of achieving a 1% cost reduction

in the next month that is July 2026.

While discussing on the ways to achieve the 1% cost reduction target, following

are the suggestions received from different employees:

1. Raj an engineer in the design department suggests that if a particular part

of the bike’s engine can be redesigned, the time required to assemble the

bike can reduce by 0.1% of the current time taken.

2. Stanley the procurement manager suggests switching to another supplier,

which will enable NBL procure the component at the same quality but at

lower price.

3. Chatur the production manager suggests revamping the entire

production and assembly line that can increase the scale of production.

4. Ankur in the paint shop suggests improvements to the bike painting

process that will reduce set up time while paining bikes of different colors

i.e. SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die).

In order to make the Kaizen method more effective, NBL is going to adopt the

5S methodology of improving its operations. Following are some of the

measures that are suggested:

Measure 1. Remove unnecessary items from the assembly line in order to reduce

the risk of installing the wrong component.

Measure 2. Arrange tools in “shadow boards” so that workers easily access the

tools and instantly know where they belong while replacing them.

Measure 3. Clean the workstation daily to remove any clutter, improve

workplace safety, inspect machines and tools to ensure that they function

properly.

Measure 4. Standardize processes to streamline workflow and enhance

efficiency.

Measure 5. Periodic staff training, daily monitoring and fixing accountability for

allocated areas in the workspace.

After implementation of the Kaizen costing methodology, the actual cost reduction was `500 per bike for the month of July 2026.

blocks.text: Royal Tea Limited manufactures and distributes finest quality black tea to
hotels, restaurants and retailers. The company has wide presence in tea market.
It has become one of the largest premium brands. The customers are very happy
with the finest quality of tea.
Royal Tea Limited never compromises with the quality of the tea. The aim of the
company is to deliver finest black tea to keep the customers happy. Instead of
sourcing from the open market, RTL has tied up with big tea estates for
procurement of finest tea leaves directly from the estate for processing in its
own plants. The tea leaves go through various processes like plucking,
withering, brushing, oxidizing, grading, drying, sorting and shaping etc. Then
these are packed in jars for distribution to the hotels, restaurants and retailers.
A growing market for beverages like tea has increased the number of tea
manufacturers, thereby increasing competition in the industry. Although their
tea is not of the same superior quality as RTL’s, many manufacturers offer their
tea at low prices to gain market share. Buckling to peer pressure tactics, RTL’s
management has also decided to reduce the price per kilogram of tea to match
average market prices. The aim of this price reduction is to increase the volume
of sales by making their product appealing to the mass market in particular the
retail and street shops. Higher volumes would contribute to growth of profits.
RTL has purchased prime TV time slots in order to reach out to the mass market
and advertise its products.
Prices of tea leaves has been fluctuating in the past few months. The rest of the
parameters like standard hours required for production of 1 kilogram tea,
standard labour rates are expected to be stable, other than increased
advertising expenses, which can be predicted accurately, other fixed overhead
costs are expected to be stable. In April 2026, newer budget standards were
finalized based on the recent management decisions and expected fluctuations
in market prices.
The following variances were compiled in May 2026, pertain to last month's
operations that arose as a consequence of implementation of above decisions
and revised budget standards:

decided to revise its product pricing strategy yet again. Keeping in mind the nature
of its tea, it decided to do a sensitivity analysis to understand customer behavior.

scenario:
Cellwell Technologies Ltd is a manufacturer of mobile phones. It has been an

established player in the market having launched various cell phone models in

the last 10 years. With the preference for usage of cell phones increasing, the

company has grown rapidly since inception. During the last two years, sales

have been increasing but said growth rate slows down, because competition

has increased. Cellwell Technologies has funded its operations with long term

loans and working capital loans. These are general purpose borrowings that are

not taken specifically for any project or development of specific phone model.

Yet, with interest costs being high the company is looking at ways to manage

costs in order to generate sufficient returns for its shareholders.

Cellwell Technologies plans to introduce a new smart phone model called

XXX21. The company’s research and development team has come up an

additional update feature for this cell phone model. This update feature will

periodically inform the customer using the cell phone regarding any software

update available and required for the model. By installing these updates many

existing bugs in the software model and security issues to the software can be

fixed. This process can be executed remotely with the help of the new

technology that the research and development team has developed. This

improves the cell phone performance and keeps it running smoothly. It

improves compatibility of the software with that used in other applications and

devices. It also enhances customer experience as the software update will bring

out new software features (like newest emotions / updated dictionary) that can

be used by the customer. Consequently, the general utility of the cell phone life

will improve by almost a year. There will be less complaints due to performance/

compatibility related issues.

The business development team has come up with a proposal for sales

promotion. As per this proposal, customers can trade in older model of their

cell phone in order to buy the latest XXX21 model. Due to the trade, customers

can buy the latest model XXX21 at a substantial discount.

After discussion with the research and development team, they conclude that

this would have an additional advantage. Cellwell Technologies can refurbish

(repair and renovate) these older model’s set and resell them in the market at

a substantially lower price. Typically, students or customers in the lower income

groups who look for cheaper cellular sets may consider these refurbished sets.

This creates a new market space for the company to target.

Those older cell phones traded in, that cannot be refurbished or are beyond

repair / use can be recycled. Various parts of these phones can be recycled and

fitted into the new cell phone models that are being made. Any cell phone

model contains various precious metals like gold, copper, platinum as also rare

earth elements like neodymium, terbium to name a few. These are difficult to

obtain and need to be mined out of the earth causing huge damage to the

environment. With the demand for cell phone slated to increase, the need for

these materials is going to increase as well. Therefore, if these materials can be

extracted from old cell phones that would otherwise have been disposed-off

into a landfill, it would benefit the environment while providing an alternate

source of material procurement. Since billions of cell phones are used globally,

every small measure makes an impact. With increasing awareness about

environment hazards, such a proposal is likely to find support among

customers. It is also helpful to improve the company’s brand image.

Both proposals to introduce software upgrade feature in the cell phone as well

as the trade in sales promotional offer are unique as no other competitor has

such features in their products or have made such offers yet. Yet, competition

is very high in this price sensitive market. Therefore, the company wants to

calculate the target cost for Model XXX 21.

Cellwell plans to offer Model XXX 21 at ` 30,000 per unit and wishes to earn a

profit of 25% on the selling price. The cost of components is `15,000 per unit;

Labor time is 0.6 hours at ` 5,000 per hour and product specific overheads

amount to ` 2,500 per unit. In addition, each phone requires 0.45 kgs of other

materials, which are supplied at a cost of ` 5,000 per kg with a normal 10%

substandard quantity, which is not usable in the manufacture.

blocks.text: All Yours Limited (AYL), an apparel manufacturing company has a factory in
Ahmedabad, making denim clothing for customers of all ages. It sells its
clothing from its factory outlet store located within the city. Until 6 months
back, the company had a business model wherein the products manufactured
at its factory would be sent to its factory outlet store. Customers would visit the
store and choose apparel suiting their tastes. Production was based on
prediction of customer demand. This “made to stock” model has been placed
for many years.
Few months back, the store manager noticed many customers exiting without
making any purchases. Tracking this and after obtaining feedback from
customers over sometime, it was found that many products were unacceptable
to the customers’ tastes - either the shade or design of denim was not what
they wanted or that the apparel was not of the correct fit for them. The
management then decided to provide customers a choice of either choosing
from their standard apparel range that has already been made (“made to stock”
model) or to offer them a “made to order” option.
The company now displays its range of denim material at the factory outlet.
Customers can go through the samples and choose the material of their choice.
Company certified tailors would then take measurements based on the
customers’ preferences. A detailed order customized to the customers’ needs
would then be drawn up. The factory has set up a separate tailoring division
that would stich the apparel specifically for these “made to order” sales. To
facilitate this, new machines and production line resources have been put in
place.
Customized products are manufactured and be made available to the customer
within 3 working days’ time from the date of placing the order. The customer
comes to the store and picks up the apparel ordered. For delays beyond this
timeline, the customer gets to pay 5% less on the order value. This is done to
attract and maintain customers, who would otherwise choose to purchase
apparel offered by rival competitors. Therefore, speed of delivery of the
customized product is critical for the company. This is the main selling point for
the company to operate the “made to order” business model.
If further modifications are needed due to errors on part of the company
(quality / finishing issues), the apparel would need to be modified / re-stitched
once again. The company will bear the cost of modification or replacement of
garment.
This new “made-to-order” has been in place for the past 6 months. At the stage
of project proposal, the management found it a lucrative option for the
company because:
(i) Customers are willing to pay a higher price to have customized clothing
as compared to the standard fitting.
(ii) It would attract more customers to the store
(iii) If the model works well, the dependence on the “made to stock” model
can reduce. Savings in inventory stock, obsolescence and warehousing
costs will benefit the company’s bottom-line.
Customers have been very enthusiastic in availing this customization facility
offered by the company. Sales have increased manifold in the last few months.
The management is interested to understand the metrics related to their “made
to order” business mode to assess its success and risks. Some of the
non-financial metrics are:

scenario:
DIC is one of several insurance companies operating in the general insurance

segment, offering policies such as car, home, accident, theft, and health

insurance. Over the past three years, DIC has experienced a steady increase in

business volumes; however, profits have remained largely static due to

declining margins. Given the high level of competition in the industry, cost

efficiency has become a critical factor for success.

Some of the key business processes at DIC are described below:

Customer consultation involves sales staff visiting potential customers at their

homes to promote and sell insurance policies. During these interactions,

customers seek information regarding premiums, policy features, and benefits.

The sales staff currently calculate premium quotations manually. The company

has observed frequent errors in this process, with the final premium charged at

the time of policy issuance differing by up to 5% from the premium originally

quoted to the customer.

Policy issuance refers to the preparation and delivery of the finalized insurance

policy. Employees are required to ensure that all policies comply with applicable

legal and regulatory requirements. A dedicated internal audit officer conducts

a final compliance check before the policy is dispatched. Any errors identified

during this review require rework, resulting in a delay of approximately one

hour for each affected policy.

Claim settlement involves the processing and payment of claims following the

occurrence of an insured loss. It has been observed that, at the time of claim

processing, essential documents—such as the insured’s tax identification

number or address proof—are sometimes missing from the file. Claims cannot

be settled until all required documentation is submitted, leading to delays of

up to one week in claim settlement.

These processes are handled by separate departments, each of which

independently records customer information in its own systems. There is no

centralized customer database, resulting in duplication of data entry and

operational inefficiencies.

To address these errors and inefficiencies, DIC has initiated the implementation

of a standardized digital operating model using various information technology

tools. In addition, several Six Sigma methodologies are being adopted to

enhance process accuracy, reduce errors, and improve processing speed.

The general insurance industry is highly regulated and requires insurers to

obtain and maintain appropriate licenses. Many insurance products, particularly

car and home insurance, are largely standardized, offering limited scope for

differentiation. Furthermore, actuaries and fintech professionals are scarce in

the labour market. The increasing use of digital platforms has enabled

customers to easily compare offerings across insurers, while low switching costs

have further intensified competition. It has also been observed that investors

increasingly view mutual funds, real estate, and gold as alternative risk

management strategies.

In response to these challenges, DIC has decided to diversify into niche

insurance segments, such as coverage for high-value collectibles and artifacts,

including fine art paintings, jewelry, watches, and other unique items. Although

a significant number of high-net-worth individuals own such assets, this market

segment remains underserved by existing insurance providers. To support this

diversification, DIC is reskilling its existing workforce to manage these

specialized products. This new line of business requires advanced underwriting

expertise and the use of blockchain technology to track and authenticate

insured assets.

one unit of Model 101 is presented below.
Recently, the market has been inundated with bookshelves made of particle
wood, which are significantly cheaper than metal bookshelves. These
competing products are sold through the same retail channels used by Durable
Limited.
To remain competitive, the Head of Marketing has proposed a 12.5% reduction
in the current selling price. At present, the company earns a pre-tax profit
margin of 10% of the selling price per unit, and management is keen to maintain
this margin in the coming years. To achieve the required cost reduction while
preserving profitability, the Chief Engineer has recommended adopting target
costing.
The Production Manager, however, expresses concern that aggressive cost
reduction measures may compromise product quality. In response, the Quality
Control Manager suggests increasing the number of inspection staff so that
inspections can be carried out at each stage of production. Early detection of
defects, according to him, would help minimise rework costs and maintain
quality standards.
The Management Accountant points out that Model 101 is a mature product,
and therefore a substantial portion of its production costs are already
committed in nature. In his view, price reductions may be difficult to sustain
and could adversely affect both the company’s top line and bottom line.
Countering this, the Chief Engineer proposes redesigning Model 101 to reduce
costs. However, the Marketing Manager resists this suggestion, arguing that the
product’s existing design, appearance, and features are the key drivers of its
popularity and sales in the market.
Given the resistance to product redesign, the Quality Control Manager
ultimately suggests that the company consider adopting Kaizen costing to
achieve continuous, incremental cost reductions without altering the product’s
core features.

scenario:
Roadrunner is a trucking company engaged in transporting commercial goods

for businesses to various ports within the state of Gujarat. These ports act as

transit points from where goods are shipped by sea to their final destinations.

Operating in a highly competitive environment, the company emphasizes

maintaining high standards of delivery quality to strengthen its market position.

All outbound shipments handled by Roadrunner involve transporting goods

from multiple locations within Gujarat to designated ports. On the return leg of

a trip, the company attempts to secure shipments from the port to major

destinations within the state. In the trucking industry, a vehicle travelling

without any cargo is referred to as “deadheading.” Since deadhead kilometres

generate costs without corresponding revenue, the company seeks to minimize

such unproductive travel. To address this, Roadrunner employs ground-level

agents who identify suitable return shipments within a short period, thereby

improving truck utilization and overall operational productivity.

To date, Roadrunner has operated exclusively under a “Full Truck Load” (FTL)

model. Under this arrangement, the entire truck is reserved for a single

customer’s consignment, which is transported directly from the customer’s

location to the destination port. FTL shipments offer several advantages,

including minimal handling of goods, single-point loading and unloading,

faster delivery times, and reduced risk of damage.

Historically, the company has benefited from low fuel prices, which supported

healthy profit margins. However, rising fuel costs in recent months, coupled

with an economic slowdown, have led to stagnation in shipment volumes and

the likelihood of smaller shipment sizes in the future. In response, Roadrunner

plans to introduce a “Less than Truck Load” (LTL) service alongside its existing

FTL operations. Under the LTL model, individual consignments are too large for

parcel carriers but insufficient to justify a full truckload. Shipments from

multiple customers are consolidated at a common collection point and

transported together to a common destination port. Customers are typically

willing to accept delivery within a maximum of seven days from the time the

goods are handed over. This model enables the company to transport smaller

consignments at a more economical cost, thereby enhancing operational

flexibility. However, to ensure full capacity utilization, the company must

expand its customer base.

The introduction of LTL services represents a strategic shift for Roadrunner.

Currently, the company handles approximately 15% of the total consignments

shipped from within Gujarat to various ports and aims to maintain, and if

possible, increase this market share amid intense competition. To attract a

broader clientele, Roadrunner has increased its advertising and promotional

efforts, including advertisements in trade publications, memberships on online

trucking load boards, and participation in industry association events. The

company intends to target mid-sized businesses that can provide shipment

volumes at regular intervals.

Senior management has established the following financial performance

targets:

a. A cost per kilometre of `200

b. Revenue per kilometre of `350

c. An average accounts receivable collection period of 10 working days

d. An average customer lifetime value of `20 lakh or more, focusing on mid-

sized customers with recurring shipment requirements

While management has traditionally concentrated on financial performance, it

now recognizes the importance of tracking non-financial operational metrics to

sustain and enhance long-term performance. To evaluate the effectiveness of

the LTL system, the following non-financial operational measures are proposed:

a. Number and percentage of customer claims for damaged goods under

the LTL system

b. Average time taken to resolve customer claims (in days from the date of

claim)

c. Percentage of LTL shipments delivered later than the agreed delivery

timeline

d. Number of days trucks remain idle due to maintenance issues or

insufficient load

e. Average time required to accumulate a full truckload under the LTL model

(in days)

f. Deadhead kilometres, representing distances travelled without cargo

g. Number of orders rejected due to non-availability of trucks

h. Percentage of LTL shipments delivered within seven days from receipt of

goods

As a management consultant to the company, you are required to analyse the

above information using the Performance Pyramid model proposed by Lynch

and Cross.

scenario:
Volt Hydropower Development Corporation Limited (VHDCL) developed the

Volt Dam Project in Yazali, Arunachal Pradesh. The project began in the

year 2003. The project was originally scheduled to be commissioned in 2007,

but due to delays, on account of disputes in land acquisition, protests by

environmentalists and political uncertainty, the project could be completed only

in 2012, five years late from the originally planned commencement date. It has

built a 1,900 MW Volt Hydro Power Complex, that comprises of Volt Dam &

1,000 MW Volt Hydro Power Plant (250MW×4), Ujwal HEP (400 MW), and Volt

PSP (500 MW).

The Volt Dam is built across the Brahmaputra River. At a height of 1,000 ft, it is

a large sized multi-purpose high rock and earth-fill embankment dam, with a

reservoir capacity of 5 cubic kilometers. The 1,000 MW Volt Hydro Power Plant

was commissioned in 2012, with variable speed features which can optimize the

round-trip efficiency under varying water levels in its reservoirs. Power is

distributed to 7 northern eastern states, including home state Arunachal

Pradesh. This will also afford irrigation to the northeastern states and also

supply millions of gallons of drinking water per day for around 2 million people

living in this region. The company earns revenue by providing power to the

north eastern states. As per agreement, home state Arunachal Pradesh gets 20%

of the generated power free. The total expenditure for this project was USD 4.5

billion; it has substantially increased by 120% due to project delays. Since

2012-13, which was the first year of operation, VHDCL has been a profit-making

company. The union government owns 75% stake and the state government

owns balance 25% stake of VHDCL.

The Volt Dam has been the object of protests by environmental organizations

and local people of the region. The protest was against the displacement of

town inhabitants and environmental consequences of the weak ecosystem. An

estimated 4,000 families have been displaced on account of this project. It led

to protracted legal battles over resettlement rights and, ultimately, resulted in

the project's delayed completion despite the fact that land acquisition was

started in 2003. However, as per the latest reports all the displaced families have

been resettled in the New Volt Township area that has been developed nearby.

The resettlement efforts started as early as 2004 at the commencement of the

project. The company could acquire alternate land at a much cheaper rate as

compared to current market prices. This township has now become a semi-

ultra-modern hill station with good road connectivity, healthcare and education

facilities. All this was done as part of the project cost. The increased economic

activity around this township created alternate employment for the local

population. Despite all these measures few human rights activists claim that

around 100 families who also lost their land to the dam project have not been

officially recognized to be part of the group entitled to resettlement benefits.

Environment activists have sounded alarm over the scale of the project as it is

an earthquake prone area. They have also raised concerns about the riverine

ecosystem that lives within the river. Following this the project came under

intense public and governmental scrutiny; this forced the management to

include adequate safeguards. The company’s engineering team modified the

design and construction of the dam keeping in mind the fragile ecosystem and

risk of earthquakes in that region. This improved the dam’s sustainability to

withstand these risks and improved its longevity.

The project has received the requisite regulatory certifications and has won

many international awards. The company has also undertaken many CSR

initiatives to conserve the surrounding fragile forests and natural ecosystem,

soil and water conservation through rain water harvesting, building rural

infrastructure and making health and education affordable to locals.

scenario:
Power Gym (PG) is a chain of boutique fitness studios offering “Holistic

Wellness” solutions to its members. It operates 15 branches across Mumbai and

provides regular gym and fitness workout services. A growing health-conscious

population, coupled with increasing aspirations to look fit and stylish, presents

strong growth opportunities for PG. However, competition in the fitness

industry is intense. In addition to its core gym and fitness offerings, and in

response to emerging consumer trends, PG plans to pivot towards providing

nutrition and diet-related services under the brand name Nutriplan.

PG is a closely held partnership firm. The partners target a Return on Equity

(ROE) of 18%, with revenues generated primarily from gym membership fees.

The chain has been in operation for the past eight years. Owing to the high

quality of its training services, PG has achieved a customer retention rate of

82%, significantly higher than the industry average of 75%. Over the last four

years, the company has added new members at an average annual growth rate

of 4%.

Customer satisfaction surveys conducted across various branches report an

average score of 4.2 out of 5, indicating strong performance. Trainers at PG are

well qualified and provide professional coaching to help members achieve their

personal fitness goals in a safe and healthy manner. Considering the long

commute times in a city like Mumbai, some customers prefer online workout

sessions. Accordingly, PG offers both in-person and online fitness classes.

Payments for these services can be made either in cash or through cashless

modes such as digital wallets.

PG has also developed a mobile application integrated with customers’

wearable technologies such as smartwatches and smartphones. These devices

track vital statistics including heart rate, calories burnt, and body weight, and

provide comparative workout performance data over time. Based on this data,

trainers suggest ways to improve individual members’ performance. The

proposed Nutriplan service is intended to complement this workout ecosystem.

Trainers and studio managers will promote personalised nutrition and diet

recommendations aligned with members’ fitness goals, including protein

supplements, health and energy drinks, and healthy snacks.

To improve profitability, the partners have decided to increase the monthly gym membership fee from `8,000 to `10,000. Studio managers and lead trainers at

all locations have been assigned targets to increase the annual growth rate of

new customers to 7% and improve customer retention to 90%. These uniform

targets apply to all studios across Mumbai. However, demand for gym and

fitness services is not evenly distributed across different suburbs. While these

targets may be easier to achieve in affluent and densely populated areas,

studios located in sparsely populated or middle-income suburbs may find them

difficult to meet.

At present, machine downtime at each gym studio averages 8% of total working

time. The partners aim to reduce this downtime to 2% in order to improve

machine utilisation. However, since certain machines require a minimum

amount of downtime for routine maintenance and repairs, some staff members

doubt whether this target is feasible.

Currently, all employees receive a fixed monthly remuneration. The partners

now intend to introduce a variable pay system linked to staff performance. Non-

training staff will receive bonuses based on feedback from their superiors.

Trainers will be eligible for bonuses based on quarterly feedback from members,

but variable pay will be awarded only if the member confirms that their personal

fitness goal for the quarter has been achieved. Several trainers have expressed

concerns about this policy, highlighting that achievement of fitness goals may

depend on factors beyond their control, such as members’ dietary habits

outside gym hours. This has led to dissatisfaction and resentment among the

training staff.

The above strategies and performance measures were implemented in April

2026. By the end of the financial year 2026–27, PG achieved the targeted ROE

of 18%. However, increased attrition among trainers led to a decline in customer

retention to 70%. This was accompanied by a drop in the customer satisfaction

score to 3.4 by the end of the year.

scenario:
Caregiver Hospital is a multi-specialty, for profit hospital in a mid-sized town.

Most of the community members have their livelihood linked with the hospital.

Many of them are directly employed at the hospital as doctors, nursing staff,

lab technicians or as other support staff. While, other may be suppliers of

medical devices, drugs to the hospital, catering or housekeeping contractors for

the hospital. Hence, existence of the hospital is vital to the community.

Growing awareness about sustainable business prompted the management to

identify areas that can help the hospital operate in a sustainable manner that

would be mutually beneficial to the organization as well as the town that

depends on it. Therefore, it has identified the following initiatives that have

been put in place to create a sustainable business. Information captured from

various departments are being considered to prepare the triple bottom line

report that is for the consumption both to internal and external stakeholders.

Identify, which of the following aspects need to be reported in the triple bottom

line report and under which of the three categories. Provide reasons for

classifying the aspect under a specific category, if applicable. Assume all aspects

are material enough to be reported in the triple bottom line report.

a. Medical staff conduct charity camps every month. Open to all members of

the community, who are provided with consultation free of charge.

b. Prompt and accurate tax payments based on records maintained without

errors or fraud.

c. Medical waste is discarded at a landfill in a nearby dumpsite. Some of the

wastes are not bio-degradable.

d. During review of the supplier for housekeeping service, it was observed

that the service provider resorted to child labor to keep cost of operations

lower.

e. Training and professional development programs for doctors and nurses.

f. Lab reports are being made available online within the hospital computer

system. This would reduce printing costs and storage space needed to

maintain older records.

g. Payment to non-medical staff lower than national average.

h. Emergency department is getting new critical care equipment. This would

reduce average wait time of patients by 30 minutes.

i. The hospital is planning to market “medical check-up packages” so that

facilities in its outpatient departments can be utilized better.

scenario:
KD Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation (KRCTC) operates one of the

largest public-facing service systems in the world, handling millions of

passengers daily across ticketing, catering, tourism, and online services. Despite

technological advancements such as online ticket booking and e-catering

platforms, KRCTC faced persistent service quality challenges, system outages

during peak booking hours, inconsistent food quality, poor grievance redressal,

and low frontline employee accountability.

Customer complaints on social media surged, audit reports highlighted process

inefficiencies, and public perception increasingly linked KRCTC with

inconvenience rather than service excellence. Senior management realized that

piecemeal fixes were insufficient; what KRCTC needed was a Total Quality

Management (TQM) transformation embedded across the organization.

The first step was top management commitment, aligning with both the Six Cs

of TQM and Deming’s philosophy. A clear quality vision— “Passenger First,

Process Always” was communicated across zones and departments. Leadership

moved away from a blame culture towards system-based problem solving,

consistent with Deming’s emphasis on improving processes rather than policing

individuals.

To build a quality-oriented culture, KRCTC trained employees and vendors on

service standards, food safety, and customer handling. Quality circles were

introduced at major stations to encourage co-operation between operations,

IT, catering partners, and customer service teams. This cross-functional

collaboration reduced departmental silos and improved issue resolution speed.

Using the PDCA Cycle, KRCTC adopted a structured improvement approach. In

the Plan phase, root causes of booking failures and food complaints were

identified using complaint analytics. In the Do phase, pilot solutions such as

server capacity upgrades, standardized food menus, and vendor rating systems

were rolled out on select routes. During Check, performance metrics like

complaint frequency, on-time grievance resolution, and passenger satisfaction

scores were monitored. Finally, in the Act/Control stage, successful practices

were standardized nationwide, supported by dashboards and periodic audits.

Customer focus has become central to decision-making. Feedback from

passengers directly influenced service redesign, while control mechanisms

including SOPs, vendor penalties, and real-time monitoring, ensured

consistency. Over time, KRCTC witnessed reduced complaints, improved public

trust, and measurable service reliability, demonstrating how TQM can transform

even complex public-sector systems.

scenario:
ConnectTel Ltd., a large Indian telecom service provider, had built its growth

strategy on rapid customer acquisition. By FY 2024, it serviced over 120 million

subscribers, of which nearly 75% were prepaid customers. Management proudly

reported steady growth in subscriber numbers; however, profitability per

customer was steadily declining.

At a quarterly review, the CFO highlighted a puzzling trend: despite higher

revenues from postpaid customers, overall margins were under pressure.

Traditional profitability analysis based on average revenue per user (ARPU) was

failing to explain the gap. The CEO therefore commissioned a Customer

Account Profitability (CAP) study using Activity-Based Costing (ABC) to

understand the true cost-to-serve different customer segments.

The internal team identified two dominant segments i.e. Prepaid Subscribers

and Postpaid Subscribers. While prepaid users generated lower ARPU, they

mostly relied on digital channels for recharges, billing information, and service

requests. Postpaid customers, on the other hand, enjoyed higher ARPU but

demanded priority services, including frequent call-centre interactions,

customized billing queries, roaming support, retention discounts, and manual

credit control processes.

Key service activities were mapped: SIM activation, network usage, billing and

collections, customer complaint handling, retention offers, and account

management. ABC analysis revealed that postpaid customers consumed

disproportionately higher resources per account- especially in complaint

resolution, credit risk management, and personalized service handling.

Further, a subset of postpaid customers with high complaint frequency and

delayed payments turned out to be marginally profitable or even loss-making

after allocating activity costs. In contrast, digitally active prepaid customers

showed lower service costs and surprisingly stable contribution margins.

The findings triggered a strategic debate at the board level. Should ConnectTel

aggressively push postpaid plans for revenue visibility, or should it redesign

service levels and pricing to reflect actual cost-to-serve? Should high-

maintenance customers be retained at all costs, or should differentiated pricing

and service rationalization be introduced?

The case forced management to confront a fundamental insight: not all high-

revenue customers are profitable, and not all low-revenue customers destroy

value. CAP using ABC had shifted the focus from “How much revenue do

customers generate?” to “How much value do they actually create?”

scenario:
Dubai Uni Limited (DUL), one of India’s largest FMCG companies, operates in

an intensely competitive market where products such as soaps, detergents, and

shampoos are highly price-sensitive. Once a product is launched, DUL faces

limited flexibility to increase selling prices due to competition and consumer

expectations. Therefore, management continuously focuses on post-launch

cost reduction without compromising quality or brand perception, a philosophy

deeply rooted in Kaizen Costing.

In one of its flagship soap brands, DUL noticed that although manufacturing

processes were efficient, packaging costs per unit were steadily increasing due

to rising raw material prices and transportation costs. Packaging constituted

nearly 18% of the total product cost, making it a critical focus area for

improvement. Rather than undertaking a major redesign or capital-intensive

overhaul, DUL adopted a Kaizen-based approach, emphasizing small,

continuous, and employee-driven improvements.

A cross-functional Kaizen team was formed, comprising members from

packaging design, procurement, logistics, quality assurance, and marketing. The

team conducted a detailed analysis of the existing packaging process and

identified multiple improvement opportunities. These included reducing the

thickness of plastic wrappers marginally without affecting durability, optimizing

carton dimensions to improve truck utilization, and minimizing the number of

colours used in printing while maintaining brand visibility.

Each improvement individually appeared insignificant. However, when

implemented consistently across millions of units, the cumulative impact

became substantial. Over a six-month period, DUL achieved a 3–4% reduction

in packaging cost per unit, without any adverse impact on product quality,

customer satisfaction, or regulatory compliance. Importantly, these

improvements were not one-time cost cuts but were institutionalized as

standard operating procedures, ensuring sustainability.

The Kaizen initiative also had behavioral benefits. Shop-floor employees and

junior managers became active contributors to cost management, shifting the

mindset from “cost control by finance” to “cost consciousness across the

organization.” Management deliberately avoided aggressive cost targets,

focusing instead on continuous monthly improvements, reinforcing the essence

of Kaizen Costing.

This case illustrates how Kaizen Costing enables organizations to systematically

reduce costs during the production phase, particularly in high-volume, low-

margin industries like FMCG, where incremental efficiency gains translate into

significant strategic advantage.

scenario:
In 2009, urban mobility across the world followed a familiar pattern—customers

hailed licensed taxis, supply was limited by permits, pricing was largely fixed,

and service quality varied widely. In this context, Kuber entered the market not

as a transport company, but as a technology platform that connected riders

with independent drivers. Crucially, Kuber owned no cars, employed no drivers,

and yet aimed to solve a deeply entrenched urban problem: inefficient and

unreliable transportation. Despite being asset-light, Kuber remained capital-

intensive in terms of technology, data infrastructure, and incentives.

Kuber’s hyper-disruptive power lay in its platform vs ownership model. By

leveraging smartphone penetration, GPS, and real-time data, Kuber matched

demand and supply dynamically. Drivers could monetize idle assets (their cars),

while customers benefited from shorter wait times, transparent pricing, and

cashless payments. Surge pricing, though controversial, ensured availability

during peak demand—something traditional taxi systems struggled to achieve

due to rigid fare structures and regulatory caps.

As Kuber scaled rapidly across cities and countries, it bypassed traditional entry

barriers such as fleet ownership, taxi medallions, and depot infrastructure.

Instead, growth was driven by network effects: more drivers reduced wait times,

attracting more riders, which in turn attracted more drivers. Venture capital

funding enabled Kuber to subsidize rides and incentives, accelerating adoption

but delaying profitability.

However, the same hyper-disruptive model also created systemic tensions.

Regulators questioned whether Kuber was a technology intermediary or a de

facto transport operator. Driver classification as independent contractors led to

legal disputes over wages, benefits, and job security. Traditional taxi unions

protested the uneven regulatory playing field. Internally, Kuber faced

governance challenges as aggressive growth targets overshadowed

compliance, culture, and stakeholder management.

By the late 2010s, Kuber stood at a strategic crossroads. Its platform model had

undeniably reshaped global mobility, but questions remained about long-term

sustainability, regulatory legitimacy, and unit economics once subsidies were

withdrawn. Kuber’s case illustrates how hyper-disruptive business models can

unlock massive value rapidly—while simultaneously amplifying strategic,

ethical, and governance risks.

scenario:
In the late 1990s, Ketflix began as a DVD-by-mail rental company, challenging

traditional video rental stores with a subscription-based model and no late fees.

For nearly a decade, this model delivered steady growth. However, by the mid-

2000s, rapid advancements in broadband technology and changing consumer

preferences signaled a fundamental shift in how entertainment would be

consumed. Ketflix’s top management realized that the future lay not in physical

media, but in digital streaming - a transition that required far more than a

change in technology; it demanded a complete organizational realignment.

At the strategy level, Ketflix made a bold decision to cannibalize its own DVD

business and invest heavily in streaming infrastructure and digital content

licensing. This long-term strategic bet prioritized scalability and convenience

over short-term profitability. To support this, the company redesigned its

structure, moving away from process-heavy functional hierarchies toward a

flatter, agile organization that empowered teams to make fast decisions.

The transition also required radical changes in systems. Ketflix invested in

advanced data analytics, recommendation algorithms, and cloud computing to

deliver seamless streaming experiences. Traditional performance controls were

replaced with outcome-focused metrics emphasizing innovation, customer

engagement, and learning speed.

Reinforcing these hard elements were critical shifts in the soft elements of the

7S framework. Ketflix consciously reshaped its shared values, emphasizing

innovation, freedom, and responsibility. These values were codified in its now-

famous culture deck, which acted as a guiding compass during uncertainty.

Leadership style moved toward high trust and transparency, where managers

acted as context-setters rather than controllers.

The company also reassessed Its staff philosophy. Ketflix adopted a “talent

density” approach - hiring fewer but exceptionally capable employees and

offering top-of-market compensation. This was complemented by the

development of new skills, particularly in software engineering, data science,

and digital content strategy, which were essential for competing in a

technology-driven entertainment industry.

While the transition involved missteps, such as the poorly communicated

Kwikster spin-off (short-lived spin-off brand announced by Ketflix), the overall

alignment of the seven elements enabled Ketflix to pivot successfully. By

ensuring that strategy, structure, systems, and culture reinforced one another,

Ketflix transformed itself from a DVD rental company into a global streaming

leader, demonstrating the power of holistic organizational alignment

envisioned by McKinsey’s 7S Framework.

scenario:
Niju’s began as a modest test-preparation venture and rapidly transformed into

India’s most celebrated ed-tech unicorn. Fueled by venture capital, aggressive

acquisitions, and a compelling vision of “making learning engaging,” the

company prioritized scale at unprecedented speed. Subscriber numbers surged,

revenues multiplied, and Niju’s soon expanded globally through acquisitions

such as Smith Jr. and Prakash. From a financial perspective, growth metrics

appeared impressive, valuation climbed, topline expanded, and investor

confidence remained high.

However, beneath the surface, cracks began to emerge. Customers increasingly

complained about aggressive sales practices, misleading free-trial conversions,

and complex refund processes. While enrolments were high, student

engagement and learning outcomes - key customer success indicators - were

not consistently tracked. The customer perspective of performance was

becoming misaligned with the company’s growth narrative.

Internally, Niju’s processes were stretched. Rapid onboarding of sales staff,

decentralized content integration from acquisitions, and weak internal controls

led to operational inconsistencies. Compliance reporting, audit closures, and

financial transparency lagged behind expansion. From a process perspective,

efficiency and governance were sacrificed in favour of speed, creating execution

risk.

The learning and growth dimension revealed deeper issues. While the company

attracted top talent and technologists, high attrition in sales teams, pressure-

driven cultures, and leadership bandwidth constraints weakened organizational

capability. Training focused more on sales targets than ethical selling or learner

outcomes. Leadership incentives were largely tied to growth metrics, reinforcing

short-termism.

As regulatory scrutiny increased and investor patience waned, Niju’s leadership

faced a strategic inflection point. The question was no longer how fast the

company could grow, but whether its growth model was sustainable. A

Balanced Scorecard review revealed a classic imbalance: financial metrics were

over-optimized, while customer trust, process discipline, and organizational

learning were under-measured and under-managed.

For Niju’s, restoring balance meant redefining success- linking financial

recovery to customer outcomes, strengthening internal governance, and

rebuilding a learning-oriented culture. The case highlights how absence of a

truly balanced scorecard can turn rapid growth into strategic vulnerability.

scenario:
By 2023, Zwigato had evolved into a highly decentralized organization,

operating across more than 500 Indian cities. To drive rapid market penetration,

the company structured its commercial operations around city-wise sales

teams, each headed by a City Business Head (CBH). These teams were

responsible for onboarding restaurants, driving order volumes, increasing Gross

Merchandise Value (GMV), and executing local marketing campaigns.

Zwigato’s top management believed that proximity to local markets would

improve responsiveness. Accordingly, each city team was evaluated primarily

on revenue growth metrics - order volume growth, GMV targets, and number

of active restaurant partners. Centralized functions such as delivery logistics,

technology platform, customer support, and national advertising were

controlled at the head-office level, and their costs were allocated uniformly

across cities.

Initially, the structure delivered impressive results. Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities

showed double-digit revenue growth, and CBHs aggressively pursued

discounts, free delivery schemes, and restaurant-funded promotions to boost

volumes. However, within a year, the finance team noticed a troubling pattern:

while revenue targets were being consistently met, contribution margins were

declining sharply in several fast-growing cities.

City heads argued that margin deterioration was beyond their control. Delivery

costs fluctuated due to fuel prices and rider availability, both centrally managed.

Technology and customer support costs were allocated irrespective of city

profitability. Yet, the incentive system rewarded only top-line growth,

encouraging CBHs to push high-discount campaigns even when they were

value-destructive at the organizational level.

At a leadership review meeting, the CFO raised a fundamental question: “Are

we holding managers accountable for what they can control?” Some senior

leaders proposed converting city teams into profit centres, while others argued

that CBHs lacked authority over key cost drivers and investment decisions. A

third view suggested retaining the revenue centre structure but introducing

non-financial and contribution-based metrics to balance behaviour.

As Zwigato prepared for its next phase of sustainable growth, top management

had to reassess whether its responsibility accounting system truly aligned

managerial behaviour with organizational objectives, or whether it was

inadvertently rewarding short-term volume expansion at the cost of long-term

value creation.

scenario:
Ceber is a ride-sharing company that has until recently been a market leader.

The company has operations across India. The operations have been divided

into the North, South, East and West regions, with a regional office looking into

the operations within its assigned area. Ceber has its Head Office in Gurgaon,

which is where the senior management personnel are present.

Recent reports have surfaced regarding how there have been dis-functional

operations without proper co-ordination between regional offices and the head

office. Many decisions like regional market expansion plans, remuneration to

drivers, maintenance of its fleet etc. have been taken in a haphazard manner,

without senior management oversight. Dis-satisfied customers leading to loss

of brand value, high churn of drivers due to non-conformance to service

contracts, run-down fleet of cars are some key factors that led to decline in

business and market share for the company. Unhappy investors forced the

resignation of the CEO and appointed Mr. Tim as the new CEO of the company.

These issues directly impact Ceber's revenue, profitability, and possibly

shareholder value. The scandals and negative publicity may have led to reduced

revenue, increased costs due to legal fees or operational challenges and

decreased profitability. The new CEO of Ceber Mr. Tim has a big job on his

hands. Therefore, Mr. Tim needs to focus on measures which will result into the

success and sustainability for the organization.

These issues necessitate a focused approach from Mr. Tim to stabilize the

company and restore shareholder confidence.

In this context, Economic Value Added (EVA) emerges as a crucial tool for Mr.

Tim. EVA goes beyond traditional financial metrics. This metric enables Mr. Tim

to evaluate the efficiency of Ceber's capital utilization and to prioritize

investments that promise to maximize shareholder value.

One year after Mr. Tim took charge, Ceber reported the following financial data

for the year:

• Net Operating Profit Before Interest & Taxes: `50,00,000

• Depreciation Expenses: `10,00,000

• Change in Net Working Capital: `15,00,000

• Capital Expenditure: `20,00,000

• Total Invested Capital: `250,00,000

• Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC): 12%

• Corporate Tax Rate: 30%

Mr. Tim is dedicated to assembling a staff that is committed to enhancing the

organization's success and sustainability.

scenario:
In 2021, Clod Drinks introduced a pioneering protein drink product into the

Australian market, achieving remarkable success characterized by robust sales

and a dominant market position. During this period, the company operated in

an environment marked by minimal competition and a conspicuous absence of

substitute products. The Clod Drinks team spent heavily on marketing and

advertising and product development to grow their market share.

In 2026, they noticed competitors for protein drink entering the market and

eating into their market share. This influx of competitors, along with the

emergence of alternative products, notably impacted Clod Drinks' profitability,

underscoring the influence of competitive pressures. The role of suppliers

became increasingly significant in sustaining their operations and maintaining

competitiveness. Suppliers play a crucial role in providing the necessary raw

materials and ingredients for Clod Drinks' protein drink production. However,

with the influx of rival companies into the market and the emergence of

alternative products, Clod Drinks may face challenges related to supplier power.

As Clod Drinks faces intensified competition, the demand for raw materials may

fluctuate, potentially leading to smaller quantities being purchased. This

scenario could bolster the bargaining power of suppliers, enabling them to

negotiate more favourable terms or impose higher prices on Clod Drinks.

Moreover, suppliers with limited alternatives may capitalize on their position to

exert greater influence during negotiations, especially considering Clod Drinks'

reliance on their input for production. Therefore, effectively managing supplier

relationships becomes crucial for Clod Drinks to ensure a stable supply chain,

mitigate risks associated with changing market dynamics, and sustain

competitiveness amidst heightened competition.

In response to the heightened competition and fluctuating market dynamics,

Clod Drinks could conduct scenario analysis to anticipate various outcomes and

devise strategic responses. By simulating different scenarios, such as changes

in market share, pricing strategies, or supplier negotiations, Clod Drinks can

proactively identify risks and opportunities to maintain competitiveness and

ensure a resilient supply chain. Scenario analysis enables Clod Drinks to make

informed decisions, adapt quickly to evolving market conditions, and safeguard

their market position in the face of intensified competition.

Specialized protein isolates, like pea, soy protein, have experienced price

fluctuations due to weather conditions affecting crop yields. Droughts, floods,

or other extreme weather events disrupted production, leading to increased

costs and potential shortages in the market. The price of protein isolates has

fallen dramatically in the last few years from over US$15/kg to less than

US$7/kg.

Clod Drinks is dedicated to empowering individuals to live more vibrant lives

through our science-driven approach to chilled beverages. Clod Drinks website

states “Our aim is to help people do more, feel better, and live longer”.

scenario:
Indian Petrons Ltd. (IPL) is a leading manufacturing company. Under increasing

pressure to reduce costs, to contain inventory and to improve service, IPL’s

Costing Department has recently undertaken a decision to implement a JIT

System.

The management of IPL is convinced of the benefits of their changes. But

However, the Supplies Manager Mr. Brian has voiced concerns regarding the

costing department’s decision. He said:

“We’ve been driven by suppliers for years ... they would insist that we could only

purchase in thousands, that we would have to wait weeks, or that they would only

deliver on Mondays!”

Minimum order quantities, long lead times and fixed delivery schedules, these

highlight the practical difficulties of implementing a strategic decision. The

management is convinced that implementing the JIT system is the way forward.

However, it does need to address Mr. Brian’s concerns before implementing this

decision.

scenario:
Bharat Heavy Machinery Ltd (BHML) operates in the automotive component

manufacturing industry, specialising in the production of internal combustion

engines supplied to passenger car manufacturers. The industry is characterised

by high fixed investments, intense price competition, and demand sensitivity to

vehicle prices, particularly in the mid-segment car market. OEMs (original

equipment manufacturers) frequently renegotiate prices with component

suppliers to manage their own cost pressures arising from fuel prices, regulatory

norms, and consumer affordability.

BHML currently incurs a variable cost of `4,200 per engine, comprising direct

materials, direct labour, and variable manufacturing overheads. Fixed costs are

substantial due to capital-intensive machinery and skilled manpower, making

capacity utilisation and contribution maximisation critical strategic objectives

for the company. Fixed costs are assumed to be unaffected by output decisions

within the relevant range and hence do not influence the MR = MC condition

for profit maximisation.

Market research conducted by BHML indicates that:

• At a selling price of `7,400 per engine, no orders are received, as

competing suppliers offer more cost-competitive alternatives.

• For every `400 reduction in the unit selling price below `7,400, demand

increases by two engines, reflecting high price elasticity in procurement

decisions of automobile manufacturers.

The management of BHML is currently reviewing its pricing strategy in light of:

• Under-utilised production capacity,

• Aggressive pricing by competitors,

• The need to balance short-term contribution maximisation with long-term

relationships with OEM customers.

The company wishes to determine the optimal unit selling price per engine that

will maximise its profit, taking into account the interaction between price,

demand, and contribution, while operating within the competitive dynamics of

the automotive components industry.

scenario:
Sigma Manufacturing Limited operates in a highly competitive, volume-driven

environment where equipment reliability, process efficiency and quality

consistency are critical for sustaining margins and meeting customer delivery

commitments. Machinery X-272K-300, deployed on a continuous production

line, operates on a 9-hour shift each day. During the shift, workers are entitled

to three short breaks of 10 minutes each and a meal break of 45 minutes,

resulting in planned downtime of 75 minutes. In addition, the machine

experienced unplanned equipment downtime of 30 minutes, reflecting normal

reliability challenges faced in heavy manufacturing operations.

Under standard operating conditions, the machine is designed to produce 30

parts per minute. During the shift, total output amounted to 12,240 units, out

of which 240 units were rejected due to quality-related issues. The presence of

rejections highlights the importance of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

and quality control systems in Sigma Manufacturing, where even minor

inefficiencies or defects can have a significant impact on overall productivity,

customer satisfaction and cost competitiveness.

In such capital-intensive industries, management relies on metrics like Overall

Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) to assess the combined impact of availability

losses, performance inefficiencies and quality defects. The data relating to

Machinery X-272K-300 therefore provides a practical basis for evaluating

operational effectiveness and identifying improvement opportunities aligned

with lean manufacturing and continuous improvement objectives.

scenario:
A company had nearly completed a job relating to the construction of

specialised equipment when it discovered that the customer had gone out of business. At this stage, the original cost estimate of the job was `1,75,200, of

which costs amounting to `1,48,500 had already been incurred. A further cost

of `29,700 would be required to bring the equipment to its original condition.

The company had received a progress payment of `1,00,000 from the original

customer before the business failure.

After making further searches, the company found a new customer who was

willing to take over the equipment, provided certain modifications were carried

out. The new customer required the equipment in its original condition but

without the control device and with some additional modifications. The

estimated costs of these additions and modifications included direct materials costing `1,050, direct wages of 15 man-days in Department A and 25 man-days

in Department B, variable overheads charged at 25% of direct wages in each department, and delivery costs of `1,350. Fixed overheads were absorbed at

50% of direct wages in each department.

Additional information revealed that the direct materials required for the

modification were already in stock. If these materials were not used for the

present modification, they would be utilised in another job in place of materials that would otherwise cost `2,250. Department A was working normally, and

therefore any labour engaged for this work would have to be paid at the direct wage rate of `120 per man-day. Department B, however, was extremely busy.

The direct wage rate in Department B was `100 per man-day, and the

department was currently generating a contribution of `3.20 per rupee of direct

wages. Supervisory overtime payable specifically for carrying out the modification amounted to `1,050.

The control device that the new customer did not require had an original cost of `13,500. If removed, this control device could be used in another job in place

of a different mechanism that would otherwise have to be purchased for `10,500. The dismantling and removal of the control device would require one

man-day of work in Department A.

Further, if the conversion was not carried out, some of the materials used in the

original equipment could be utilised in another contract in place of materials that would have otherwise cost `12,000. To make these materials suitable for

that alternative use, two man-days of work in Department A would have been required. The remaining materials would realise `11,400 if sold as scrap. In

addition, the drawings relating to the job, which formed part of the original work, could be sold for `1,500.

The company wishes to analyse the minimum price which it can afford to quote

to the new customer under the circumstances stated above.

margins often influenced by fluctuations in raw material prices, labour costs,

and capacity utilisation levels. Manufacturers are therefore required to take

scenario:
Payfast Limited offers an application-based service to make payments by

phone. It allows linking of phone numbers with bank and allow bank to bank

transfer through mobile phone. It was the 1st company in the country to provide

such facility. The customers could not believe that money transfer process could

be made so much simpler and the company witnessed exponential growth in

very few days. The services level was immaculate and no charges were levied on

the customers.

Seeing the growth of Payfast, many other companies joined the industry and

started providing this service. Now, Payfast was required to provide other

innovative offerings to the customers to maintain its leadership position.

Further, the user count was increasing and so Payfast was spending more to

maintain security of the user data. Security breach, if any, could result into loss

of millions to its users. Similarly, all its competitors were also spending a

significant amount on the security of user data.

In order to incentivize customers, Payfast starting giving cash backs to its

customers on every transfer. This was unheard of. Getting money for making

transfer was unheard and its competitors also could not fathom it.

To compete with Payfast, its competitors started adding new features on their

applications like movie booking, cab booking, etc. But this was not well received

by customers as customers used different apps for such transactions which had

much better user interface and features. Slowly, the competitors removed those

features and went back to their original product.

To improve its brand presence, Payfast hired an international brand designed

and designed a new logo for Payfast.

scenario:
You recently joined Management Accounting department of a fashion retail

brand ‘Paridhaan’ which is struggling with dipping margin and deficiencies

issues in distribution network. In meeting with CEO and COO you explained to

them that Supply Chain Management can be great help for Paridhaan to

overcome the many of challenges that Paridhaan is currently facing.

You explained them the case of Sara Fashions, how they manage their Supply

Chain to create value. Sara Fashions is a well-known premium international

clothing manufacturer and retailer based in Spain that was founded in the

1980s. Because of the collaborative relationship with customers and suppliers,

Sara can complete the development and shipment of a new product to its stores

in two to six weeks, which is faster than the industry average of six months. Sara

sells their clothing through its own retail outlets.

One of the most significant benefits of Sara's supply chain strategy is its ability

to respond quickly to all fashion trends and provide customers with the latest

fashion outfits in two weeks in case of existing items while 4-6 weeks in case of

new items. Secondly, Sara never produces in large quantities, so if the style does

not sell as well as expected, Sara does not suffer as much loss because there is

not much stock to discount. Thirdly, the advantages of a vertically integrated

and shortened supply chain are obvious. Sara's advertising cost is only 0.3% of

its revenue, whereas similar fashion retailers typically spend 3% on advertising

and marketing.

The high frequency of shifts in displayed merchandise (about three-quarters of

them are changed every three weeks) allows for consistent customer visits.

The main disadvantage of Sara's supply chain is that because Sara owns all of

the supply chain channels, it is difficult to expand to distant locations because

it is very expensive to distribute such products.

Sara factories in Spain employ flexible manufacturing systems to allow for rapid

changeover operations. Only 24% of all items are manufactured in Asia and

Africa, while 50% are manufactured in Spain; and rest 26% in the rest of Europe.

Sara's factories can quickly increase and decrease production rates, resulting in

less inventory in the supply chain and less need for working capital to finance

that inventory. They only do 50 to 60% of their manufacturing in advance,

compared to competitors' 80 to 90%. Sara does not need to bet heavily on

yearly fashion trends. They can place numerous smaller bets on short-term

trends that are easier to predict.

They employ approximately 3,000 workers in manufacturing operations in Spain

at an average wage of 11.00 euros per hour, compared to an average wage of

0.80 euros per hour in Asia.

COO pointed that there are two flows that majorly we need to manage

at Paridhaan; the physical flow of material and exchange of information.

Since many of terms and points are new for them hence, they shower you back

with questions which you need to answer (with most appropriate option)

keeping above facts in backdrop of your mind.

scenario:
National Fabric Company (NFC) is a leading manufacturer of Khadi fabric that

is further made up into dresses and suits. NFC was established 4 decades ago

and presently enjoys the reputation as producer of quality fabric. The popularity

of “Made in India” concept in the recent years, has led to a change in demand

for Khadi fabric. NFC manufactures this fabric in designs established many years

ago keeping its traditional nature intact. The manufactured fabric is further sold

to designing and weaving companies who would then covert them into

traditional Khadi attire like Kurtas, shirts etc. Hence, currently NFC does have

any direct reach with textile retail outlets or final customers.

Management team at NFC believe in integration (vertical and horizontal) and

diversification (product range or market reach) as tool of risk reduction and

value enhancement (profit too).

NFC opts for acquiring ‘Skylark Designer Clothing’ (SDC). SDC is operating in

designing and weaving of fabric into fashion wears which are mainly made from

modern fabric material (for example satin, chiffon, velvet etc.) that give required

gloss, style and look. SDC is targeting the customers who are in age group from

18 to 30 years. Customers in this category comprise of fashion-conscious youth,

for whom looking “in vogue” would be a desired preference. Due to its high-

quality sourcing of materials, young and dynamic fashion designers and well

spread distribution channels, SDC is a very profitable enterprise. SDC’s apparel

are sold in different retail textile outlets that have a national presence.

The management of NFC plans to retain the fashion apparel line of SDC.

However, it plans to scale up the operations of SDC to include the next age

group category of 30 to 50 years. The end user customers of this category are

generally price sensitive. However, they have higher disposable income as

compared to the age group category of 18 to 30 years. Therefore, demand is

likely to be at least 8 times more than the demand generated by the 18 to 30

year category.

Since, the scale of operations is expanding many times over, NFC plans for SDC

to manufacture its own fabric instead of relying on external vendors. For the 30

to 50 year category, the quality of fabric will be of a lower grade as compared

to the other segment. A detailed market research is undertaken to understand

the fashion apparel industry. NFC finds that there are many players in the 30-

to-50-year category. Retail outlets hence have a variety of suppliers to choose

from for the fashion apparel for the 30-to-50-year category. Hence, due to high

competition and price sensitive end users, it is difficult to negotiate a premium

price for the apparel for this category. Therefore, NFC finds the profit margin

per apparel unit in this venture will be much lower. The management of NFC

thereby concludes that due to the different market conditions, the pricing

strategies for the 18 to 30 year category and the 30 to 50 year category will be

different.

In order to increase profitability NFC plans to circumvent even the retail outlets

and reach out to the direct customers directly via online website portals. It plans

to ship the orders using private courier service. This is aimed at both the

segments of customers of SDC.
